text:
USER: correct all misspellings of names AND SHOW ME THE CORRECTED PROSE

The Great Hall was warm and loud, a fortress against the storm hammering the enchanted ceiling. The air smelled of toasted bread and oatmeal. Remus Lupin sat at the Gryffindor table, feeling surprisingly solid. It was two weeks until the moon—that golden window where the scars were faded silver and the bone-deep ache hadn't yet returned. He felt capable. He felt hungry.

He stacked a third pancake onto his plate.

"So," Sirius Black was saying, gesturing with a piece of toast, "I told Flitwick that the collision of the two desks was a commentary on the socio-economic struggles of goblin rebellion architecture."

"He was probably crying because you singed his eyebrows, Padfoot," James Potter replied.

But James's voice lacked its usual boom. He was hunched over his plate, his face a pale, waxy shade under the messy black hair. He wasn't eating. He was just staring at a sausage as if it were a poisonous beetle.

"I didn't singe them," Sirius argued. "They just... receded. In fear."

Peter Pettigrew giggled, spraying crumbs across the table.

Remus watched James. He looked wrong. There was a sheen of sweat on James's forehead that had nothing to do with the heat of the room.

"Prongs?" Remus asked quietly. "You alright?"

James blinked, looking up slowly. "Yeah," he said, his voice rough. He rubbed a hand over his face. "Actually... no. I've been feeling a bit under the weather. Since last night."

"You should go to Pomfrey," Remus said immediately.

"Nah," James waved a hand. He swallowed hard, his throat clicking. "Just need to... get some air."

The smell of the sausages seemed to suddenly saturate the space between them. James's face shifted from pale to an alarming shade of green.

"James?" Sirius leaned in.

James clapped a hand over his mouth. He doubled over.

A stream of vomit exploded across the golden platters of sausages and bacon. Splatter hit the goblets and dripped onto the tablecloth. The smell of bile cut through the warm breakfast aromas like a knife.

For a second, nobody moved.

Peter let out a high-pitched squeak and scrambled backward, nearly tumbling off the bench. Sirius shot upright, his knees catching the underside of the table and jostling it, which sent a jug of pumpkin juice tipping over. Orange liquid spread toward the edge, mingling with the mess.

"Bloody hell, Prongs!" Sirius yelped, pinching his nose. "That was visceral!"

James gasped, spitting onto the mess. He looked up, eyes watering, utterly humiliated. "Told... you..."

He swayed dangerously.

"Right, that's it," Remus said, sliding out from the bench and grabbing James's arm. "Sirius, grab his other arm. Hospital Wing. Now."

"This is why I stick to toast," Sirius muttered, though he was already gripping James's shoulder, supporting the weight with surprising gentleness. "Come on, you disaster. Let's get you to bed."

"I'm fine," James mumbled weakly, wiping his mouth on his sleeve. "Just... a bug."

"Yeah, a bug that just murdered the Gryffindor breakfast," Sirius said, steering him toward the doors. "Madam Pomfrey is going to love this."

As they moved away, a sharp swish echoed behind them. Remus glanced back. Professor McGonagall had descended from the high table, her lips pressed into a thin line. With a brusque wave of her wand, she vanished the mess in an instant. A twist of her wrist replaced the soiled tablecloth with a fresh, white one, and a new platter of sausages popped into existence, steaming and innocent, as if nothing had ever happened.

Remus turned back to the cool air of the entrance hall. It wasn't fatal, but it was messy enough to ensure James wouldn't be leaving the Hospital Wing for a week. Madame Pomfrey didn't mess around with stomach bugs.

Remus felt a strange, sharp pang of guilt. James was miserable. But Sirius would be pacing the common room within the hour, climbing the walls with boredom and worry. He would need a distraction. And Remus, for the first time in weeks, felt strong enough to be one.

The Hospital Wing smelled of liniment and starch. Madam Pomfrey took one look at James's greenish pallor and ushered him into a curtained bed with the efficiency of a field medic.

"Stomach flu," she diagnosed, waving her wand over a glass of potion that turned a violent shade of purple. "Drink this. And don't let him leave that bed for three days, minimum."

James swallowed the potion with a grimace and collapsed back against the pillows, looking pathetic.

"Try not to die, Prongs," Sirius said, backing away as Pomfrey shooed them toward the door. "We'll bring you homework."

"Don't you dare," James mumbled, already pulling the sheets up.

They sprinted down the marble staircase, taking two steps at a time. The corridors were emptying; the bell was due to ring any second.

They slid through the door of the Transfiguration classroom just as the clock on the wall began to chime. Professor McGonagall was writing on the blackboard, her back straight as a rod. She didn't turn around, but her chalk paused for a fraction of a second as they tiptoed to their usual seats in the back row.

Remus sat down, breathless. A second later, Sirius dropped into the chair beside him.

The chair on the other side of Sirius remained empty. James's usually chaotic desk was clear—no half-finished essay, no doodles of Snitches, no stack of Chocolate Frog cards.

McGonagall turned around, her spectacles glinting. She scanned the room, her gaze flickering over the empty seat.

"Mr. Black," she said. "I see Mr. Potter has decided to spare my classroom the contents of his stomach."

She turned back to the board, tapping it with her wand. The words HUMAN TO ANIMAGUS: THE PHYSIOLOGY OF TRANSFORMATION appeared in elegant, swirling script.

"Open your books to page 213," she commanded.

The rest of the day seemed to drag. Without James to whisper jokes across the History of Magic aisle or pass illicit notes during Charms, the castle felt strangely large and echoing. The other students went about their business, but the gap at the Gryffindor table was impossible to ignore.

By the time the final bell rang, the gray light of afternoon was already fading into a dreary twilight. Remus and Sirius walked back to the tower in a comfortable silence, though Sirius kept tapping his wand against his leg, a rhythmic, impatient beat.

The common room was a welcome relief of warmth and noise. The fire was roaring in the grate, casting flickering orange shadows over the worn armchairs. It was crowded, students sprawled on the rug finishing essays or huddled in corners gossiping.

Remus claimed his usual seat near the fire, sinking into the worn velvet. He opened The Dark Forces: A Guide to Self-Protection, grateful for the distraction. The words on the page blurred slightly—he was tired, but it was a normal tiredness, not the bone-crushing exhaustion of the week before.

Sirius dropped into the chair opposite him, throwing his legs over the armrest. He pulled out a roll of parchment and a quill, but instead of writing, he spent ten minutes trying to see if he could balance the inkwell on the end of his nose.

"It's going to tip," Remus said, not looking up from his chapter on counter-curses. "And you're going to stain the rug, and Filch will make you scrub it with a toothbrush."

"You have no sense of adventure, Moony," Sirius mumbled, the inkwell wobbling precariously. "It's about balance. Focus. Inner peace."

"It's about making a mess."

The portrait hole swung open with a creak, letting in a draft of cooler air. Peter Pettigrew scrambled through, looking flushed and breathless. He craned his neck, spotted them by the fire, and hurried over, dodging a pair of first-years playing Wizard's Chess.

"Well?" Sirius sat up so fast the inkwell slid off his nose and landed safely in his lap with a soft thud. "Is he still alive? Did he survive the Great Sausage Massacre?"

Peter collapsed onto the rug between their chairs, wiping sweat from his forehead. "He's asleep. Pomfrey gave him a Sleeping Draught."

"Poor Prongs," Sirius said, though a grin was tugging at the corner of his mouth. "Finally, some peace and quiet."

"He looks awful," Peter added, pulling a squashed bag of Bertie Bott's Every Flavor Beans from his pocket. "Pale as a sheet. She says he's severely dehydrated. She wouldn't let me stay—she said he needs to rest. He won't be up for dinner, and she said she's keeping him overnight. Maybe two days."

"Two days?" Sirius raised his eyebrows, tossing a bean into his mouth and grimacing—boiled cabbage flavor. "For a stomach bug? She just likes the drama of it all."

"She said he needs to be monitored," Peter mumbled, chewing rapidly. "She wasn't happy when I tried to wake him up to say hi. She practically shoved me out the door."

Dinner had been uneventful. The food was bland, the conversation sparse, and they finished quickly. They headed up to the dormitory to change.

The room was filled with the usual sounds of the evening—buckles clinking, robes hitting the floor. Sirius was stripping off his clothes with aggressive, jerky movements. Peter had turned his back to the room, changing with frantic modesty.

Sirius turned, spotted him, and let out a short, sharp, barking laugh.

"Oi, Wormtail," Sirius crowed, leaning against his bedpost with a predatory grin. "Where's your knob? Did you forget to do that bit last time you transformed back into a human?"

Remus flinched. "Keep your voice down," he hissed, crossing the room. "Do you want the whole school to hear?"

"Hear what Moony? About Peter's teeny weenie? It's not news mate."

Peter flushed a deep, miserable red, snatching his pajama bottoms and yanking them up with trembling hands. "Oh, very funny," he muttered, his voice high and tight. "I haven't heard that one a thousand times."

He thrust his hand up, extending two fingers in a rude gesture right under Sirius's nose, then turned on his heel and marched to his bed, yanking the curtains shut with a violent snap. Remus turned toward his own four-poster, reaching for the hangings, but Sirius's voice cut through the din. "Moony." He was sitting cross-legged on his mattress, the battered chessboard open in front of him. "James is sick. Fancy sitting in? I may actually be able to win one tonight."

Lupin walked back and sat at the foot of the bed. "I'm not very good. “I know,” said Serious with a laugh. “Pick a color.”

Black I suppose."

"Excellent," Sirius declared, directing the first move. The white stone soldier marched forward and halted on its square.

"Pawn to E5," Remus replied, sending the black pawn out to meet it in a mirror of aggression. Sirius drummed his fingers on his knee, a restless rhythm that matched the game. "Slow game tonight?"

"It's been a long day."

"Dull, you mean," Sirius countered, leaning forward with his chin in his hand. "Knight to F3." The white knight trotted out, carving a path through the ranks with a heavy clatter.

"Bishop to C5," Remus countered, watching the bishop slide diagonally, its robes swishing across the board. "And it wasn't boring. It was uneventful."

"Peace is boring," Sirius argued, knocking a pawn aside with his finger. "Bishop to C4." The two bishops faced each other down the diagonal, black against white.

"You just miss the noise," Remus said, studying the board. "Knight to C6." The black horse jumped forward, defending the center.

"I miss Prongs making an idiot of himself," Sirius admitted, watching the pieces closely. "He's better at being sick than we are. He makes it look heroic."

"He vomited on the sausages, Sirius. It wasn't heroic."

"It was dramatic," Sirius insisted. "Queen to H5. Check." The white queen swept down the flank, her eyes glowing.

"Knight to F6," Remus said, and his knight stepped out to block the queen.

"You're so predictable, Moony," Sirius said, leaning back against the headboard. "Defense. Always defense."

"It keeps me alive," Remus said simply, moving a pawn. "Your move."

Sirius looked up from the board, his expression one of mock-serious inquiry.

"Whatever happened with Orla Quirke?" Sirius asked abruptly. "Before she went running to Bertram Aubrey. Was she a good kisser?"

Remus felt the heat crawl up his neck. "Sirius—"

"How far did you get?" Sirius pressed, ignoring the evasion.

"Not far," Remus muttered, staring at the polished wood of the bedframe, the old shame burning fresh. "I got my hand up her shirt. That was it."

"Wait," Sirius said, his eyebrows shooting up. "You copped a feel and that was it? Did she return the favor? Didn't she even get your cock out?"

Remus shook his head quickly, his face burning. "No. She pulled away. She saw the scars, Sirius. All the ones down my side. It killed the mood instantly. And..." He gestured vaguely at his own face, his voice dropping to a miserable mumble. "it’s hard enough to talk to girls without looking like I’m about to collapse. Most of the time I look peaky. It’s not exactly an attraction, is it?"

Sirius stared at him, genuinely appalled.

"That's it?" Sirius demanded, his voice rising before he remembered where they was and lowered it to an intense whisper. "You mean to tell me the only action your knob's ever seen was a nappy change when you were a toddler? That's a tragedy, Moony. It's practically criminal neglect."

"Keep your voice down," Remus hissed, glancing nervously toward the dormitory door, though the chatter of the other boys was loud enough to cover them.

Sirius ignored him, his gaze narrowing as he studied Remus with a peculiar intensity, like he was solving a puzzle.

"It's not right," Sirius said, his voice dropping back down, low and persuasive. "You're sixteen. You're a Marauder. You shouldn't be suffering because some bird can't handle a bit of rough terrain. It's not fair."

"Life isn't fair," Remus said bitterly, moving his rook to distract himself. "Especially not mine."

"No, but we can cheat it," Sirius countered. He leaned back, his grey eyes locked on the struggling white queen in his hand. "Look, Moony. The mechanics are simple. A hand is a hand. It doesn't matter if it's a girl or... well, someone else who knows what they're doing. Nerves don't care who's touching them, do they?"

He paused, letting the suggestion hang in the air, testing the waters. Remus stiffened, his hand hovering over his knight, but he didn't pull away.

"What are you saying?" Remus asked quietly.

"I'm saying that practice helps," Sirius said with a casual shrug, though his eyes shifted to fix on Remus. "If you want to know what it feels like—really feels like, without the panic of trying to impress a bird—I could help. Just you know, nothing weird, girls practice with each other all the time. Just... a service. Between mates."

Remus stared at him as he placed the chessboard back on the nightstand, still fiddling with the indignant queen. The usual noise of the dormitory pre lights out, seemed to fade into the background.

Why am I getting hard? It's a trick.

"You're... you're having me on," Remus said, his throat dry. "Be serious, Sirius."

"No I'm not. Where's the harm? As I said, a hand is a hand and.... a mouth is a mouth. Why should it matter who's they are?"

Sirius reached out and tugged the velvet hangings shut around his four-poster. The rings clattered along the rod, effectively shielding them from view.

"Who would know?" Sirius whispered.

Remus looked at him, his mind racing. It was a trap. It had to be a trap. A practical joke. If he said yes, Sirius would laugh, mock him, call him a poof for the rest of his life.

Has to be a way to flip this... think Remus...

"Uh huh," Remus said, his voice tight. "I say yes, you call me a bender forever. I know how you operate mate. I say no and you call me a chicken forever. Lose lose. But why are you picking on me? I thought we were friends?"

"I'll call your bluff, though. If you're serious, here..." He spread his hands, gesturing at his waist. "Get it out yourself and get on with it. If not, knock it off."

Sirius stared at Remus's lap, his chest rising and falling slightly faster than before.

Remus waited five seconds. Then ten.

"Right," Remus said, the tension draining out of him, replaced by a cold flush of embarrassment. "Good joke, Padfoot."

He started to reach for the hangings.

"Muffliato."

Before Remus could react, Sirius's hands clamped around his ankles. He yanked hard.

Remus slid down the mattress, his back hitting the sheets, a grunt forced out of him as he was flattened against the bed. Sirius didn't speak. He hooked his fingers into the waistband of Remus's pajama bottoms and dragged them down to his knees in one rough motion.

The cool dorm air hit his skin.

"Padfoot, wait," Remus hissed, scrambling to sit up, his hands going to Sirius's shoulders to push him away. "Sirius, stop, you don't have to—"

Sirius ignored him. He shoved Remus's legs apart, settling between them, and lowered his head.

Remus gasped, his back arching off the mattress as he felt the wet, shocking heat of Sirius's tongue lapping his balls.

He lay rigid against the mattress, fingers clawing at the duvet, expecting the bite, the hex, the cruel laughter that had to follow. This was a line you didn't cross. But Sirius didn't stop. Sirius's tongue was relentless, dragging a low, involuntary sound from Remus's throat. It wasn't a joke. The realization settled in his gut, heavy and strange. He stopped resisting and accepted the madness of it—here, in the dark, with one of his best friends between his legs.

Then Sirius shifted.

Remus choked on a breath as Sirius's lips slid upward and swallowed the head of his cock. The shock was blinding, a violent reset of his reality. He arched his back, his entire body seizing at the sudden, hot sensation. He stared blindly at the bed hangings, his mind gone blank.

But the rhythm was steady, grounding. The panic receded, washed away by the overwhelming feeling. Remus let his head fall back, his hands unclenching from the sheets to hover uncertainly over Sirius's hair before settling on his shoulders. He stopped analyzing. He stopped worrying. He let his legs fall open farther and surrendered fully.

Remus moaned. The suction was maddening, creating a feeling that was overwhelming, dragging him under and drowning out every thought.

That was it. Remus stopped thinking. He closed his eyes, his fingers tangling in Sirius's hair, urging him on.

"Faster," Remus gasped, his voice cracking. "God, Sirius... like that… no, no, the other—yes. That. Keep doing that."

Sirius obeyed, his head bobbing with a ruthless rhythm, flicking his tongue against the sensitive bell-end every time his head rose. The pressure began to build and Remus begged him not to stop. To go faster.

Then he cried out, his back bowing off the mattress, his entire body heaving as he came. He spilled himself into Sirius's mouth, pulsing waves of pleasure that left him dizzy and trembling.

Sirius took it all with a satisfied moan. He held steady through the aftershocks, his throat working as he swallowed.

As Remus's body finally slumped back against the sheets, limp and heavy, Sirius began to pull away. As he lifted his head, he pressed his thumb against the underside of Remus's softening cock, dragging it upward to milk out the last, pearly drops.

Remus whimpered at the overstimulation, his hips jerking weakly.

Sirius sat back on his heels, wiping his mouth with the back of his hand as he watched Remus's cock pulsate and grow soft again.

The silence between them was absolute, heavy enough to suffocate. The only sound was the ragged rattle of air in Remus's own lungs. He couldn't look at Sirius. If he looked at him now, the foundations of their friendship—the only solid ground Remus had left—might finally crumble.

He fumbled with his pajama bottoms, his fingers clumsy and numb. He yanked the fabric up over his hips, covering himself with frantic, jerky movements. The elastic snapped against his waist.

Remus shifted toward the curtain. He parted the heavy velvet just a fraction, squinting into the gloom of the dormitory.

Peter's bed was a shapeless lump in the dark, the sound of his wheezing breaths steady and rhythmic. The coast was clear.

Remus slipped out from between the hangings, his bare feet hitting the cold floorboards. He didn't look back. He couldn't.

"Better luck next time, Padfoot," he mumbled, his voice hollow and flat in the quiet room.

He walked the three steps to his own bed, the distance feeling like a mile. He pulled the hangings shut with a sharp snap, sealing himself inside.

He collapsed onto the mattress, staring up at the faded, shadowy canopy. His heart was still thundering against his ribs, a frantic drumbeat that drowned out everything else. He pressed the heels of his hands against his eyes, trying to banish the image of Sirius on his knees, the phantom sensation of heat and suction still lingering on his skin. It was insane. It was impossible. But it had happened.

The darkness pressed against his eyelids, but it provided no answers. Remus lay rigid, his hands clenched into fists at his sides, fighting to slow the frantic rhythm of his heart.

What have I done?

The panic rose like bile. He had crossed a line that wasn't just invisible—it was non-existent. Friends didn't do this. Brothers didn't do this. If anyone found out—if Filch caught them, if McGonagall suspected, if James ever knew—Remus would be shipped home in disgrace. Dumbledore had bent the rules to let a werewolf into Hogwarts; he wouldn't hesitate to expel a degenerate. A bent, disgusting poof. A nancy boy.

Am I a poof?

The thought was repellent. He couldn't be. He refused to be. He was sixteen, he was hormonal, he was a freak of nature, but he wasn't that. He had spent hours staring at Lily Evans's hair, at the way Evelyn's robes fell over her shoulders. He liked girls. He wanted to marry a witch someday, have a normal life, a life where the monster was locked away once a month and the rest of the time he was just a man.

But men didn't react the way he had. Men didn't arch their backs and gasp and beg for it when their best friend touched them. He had enjoyed it. He had needed it. And that was the most terrifying part of all.

He felt sick. Shame twisted in his gut, sharp and final. He had taken advantage of Sirius's offer—a joke... he thought it was a JOKE! He was calling his bluff was all. He didn't want... never wanted.

He rolled over, burying his face in the pillow, inhaling the scent of old laundry and stale air. He wanted to scrub his skin raw. He wanted to erase the memory of Sirius's hands on his hips, the sight of him on his knees, holding him down as he took him so effortlessly.

The lingering wetness on his skin wasn't a comfort; it was evidence. The ghost of the suction felt like ash, like rot. It was a permanent mark. He could scrub his flesh until it bled, but he would still know.

A queer.

That was what they would call him. That's what happened to boys who did this. They got kicked to pieces in back alleys. They got their teeth kicked down their throats. They were permanent outcasts, broken, sick, perverted. If anyone ever found out—if Sirius snapped tomorrow and let it slip, if Peter had been awake, if James looked at him and saw the stain of it—his life was finished. Oh my god James... If he ever found out.. I'm already a monster Merlin... what have I DONE?!

Now, he'd be a target for every heavy boot and hateful fist on the street. No one would ever look at him as a human being again. He would be nothing but a dirty, diseased queer.

What if Sirius hated him now? What if Sirius had done it as some kind of sick test to see how far the freak would go?

It made him sick to his stomach. He was sixteen, and he had just signed his own death warrant. He had handed Sirius the weapon to completely destroy him.

He squeezed his eyes shut tighter, seeing the scene replay behind his lids. He opened his eyes and closed them. He shook his head against the pillow, a squeaky, choked sound escaping his throat as he mouthed the word "no" over and over, but the thing he had done wouldn't go away.

A sudden draft, sharp and biting, sliced through the warmth of the bed. Remus flinched, his eyes snapping open as a flare of wand-light ignited the darkness, momentarily blinding him. The silhouette of a figure loomed over him, blocking out the dim glow of the embers in the grate.

Before Remus could scramble away, the mattress dipped heavily. Sirius was climbing in.

"No," Remus choked out, but his voice was a thin, wasted thing.

Sirius ignored the weak protest. He reached back and yanked the velvet hangings shut with a violent swish that sent the dust motes dancing in the wand-light. Another swish, and the buzzing hum of Muffliato filled the small space, sealing them in a cone of silence.

"Let's talk," Sirius said. He sat cross-legged at the foot of the bed, his face half in shadow, his grey eyes searching Remus's.

Remus turned his face into the pillow, squeezing his eyes shut, trying to will himself out of existence. "Go away."

"REMUS! Snap out of it."

Sirius's voice was firm, cutting through the buzzing haze. He reached out and grabbed Remus’s shoulder, shaking him.

"It's ok," Sirius said, his tone shifting from command to something that sounded uncomfortably like reassurance. "It's not a big deal."

Something in Remus fractured. The casual dismissal, the ease with which Sirius spoke—it was like throwing petrol onto a fire. The sob came out of him before he could stop it, an ugly sound that he tried to muffle in the pillowcase but failed. He turned over, his chest heaving, his vision swimming with tears that blurred Sirius’s face into a grey smudge.

"Not a big deal?" Remus gasped, his voice cracking, high and terrified. He curled in on himself, hugging his knees to his chest as if he could physically hold his body together. "Are you mad? It’s... it’s everything. It’s the end. I’m a... I’m a poof, Sirius. A queer. A bender. That’s what it means, isn’t it? That’s what happens when you—when you let someone—"

"Then what does that make me... Remus...? And it's not true in any case but do continue. Get it all out, I've been where you are and I can help you get back."

Lupin sat up and stared daggers at Serious. "You have NOT been where I am!
I’m already a monster," his voice trembling so hard his teeth chattered. "One night a month I tear myself apart, and the rest of the time I’m just trying to pretend I’m human.

"You're right, YOU'RE... right. I don't know what that part's like and I'm sorry for making it sound as if I did."

I can’t be this. I can’t be that too. If Dumbledore finds out... he’ll expel me. He has to. You know he will. He bent the rules for a werewolf, but he won’t bend them for a... for a deviant. For a pervert."

He looked up at Sirius, his eyes wide and wet, pleading for understanding, for denial, for anything to stop the spinning of the room.

"My parents would disown me. They’d look at me like I’m worse than Greyback. And James... oh god, James." Remus buried his face in his hands, his fingers digging into his scalp. "If James ever found out, he’d look at me and see the filth. He’d hate me. He’d leave. All of you would. And you should. I’m sick. I’m disgusting. I let you—I enjoyed it, Sirius, and that makes me sick. It makes me wrong. I can’t be a werewolf and a... and a nancy boy. I can’t survive being both. I won't survive it."

He fixed Remus with a steady, unblinking stare. "I reckon, deep down, what nobody wants to admit is that everybody has a... curiosity, at some point. Even those who are mostly attracted to the opposite sex. I've thought about this a lot—far longer than I needed to, mostly because I didn't have anyone to make me see how it really is."

Sirius sighed, a frustrated breath escaping him. "It's not black and white, Remus. It's not a switch you flip. It's... just not. You like girls? So do I. But that doesn't mean I can't like doing... that too."

He leaned forward, his voice dropping to a conspiratorial, bitter whisper. "The trouble is, there's the way people actually are, and then there's the way the Ministry—and the Prophet, and the whole lot of them with their wands up their arses—want us to be. They want us to fit into neat little boxes, tidy and proper, like chocolates in a display case. But people aren't tidy, Moony. And the truth is usually a lot more interesting than the rules."

Remus let out a long, shaky breath, the fight draining out of him until he felt hollowed out. He scrubbed a hand over his face, his skin feeling tight and sticky. Sirius’s logic was arrogant and reckless, the sort of thing he’d say to justify setting off Filph’s firecrackers, but... it grounded him. The panic wasn't gone, but it was no longer screaming.

"It’s easy for you to say," Remus muttered, his voice rough. He kept his knees pulled up, a defensive wall between them. "You break rules for sport, Sirius. If this gets out, people will just call you a thrill-seeker.

He finally looked up, his eyes red-rimmed and wary. "You really think nobody heard? That Peter’s asleep?"

"OI, WORMTAIL! WAKE UP, YOU SODDING LUMP!"

The shout was a cannon blast in the quiet room. Remus nearly jumped out of his skin, his heart seizing in his throat. He scrambled forward, clapping his hands over Sirius’s mouth, eyes wide and darting toward the bed across the room.

He waited for the creak of springs, for the groan of a waking boy, for the catastrophe of discovery.

But from the pile of blankets across the room, Peter’s breathing didn’t falter. The same wet, rhythmic wheeze continued, undisturbed.

Sirius peeled Remus’s fingers away, looking entirely too pleased with himself.

"See? Relax," Sirius said, leaning back on his heels. "As far as Wormtail is concerned, you’re just being a sore loser because I checkmated you in under twenty moves."

Remus let out a breath he felt he’d been holding since the Great Hall. The buzzing of the Muffliato charm seemed to thicken, creating a bubble of safety around the bed.

Sirius studied him, his grey eyes piercing through the gloom. "Have you ever thought about it? About blokes?"

Remus looked away, his throat tight. The safety of the charm was one thing, but speaking the truth was another.

"It’s alright, Remus," Sirius said, his voice softer now. "I have, as you may have guessed. And I reckon, if you have, you'll feel a lot better if you just admit it. To yourself, if not to me."

He paused, letting the silence stretch, heavy and waiting. "But if you haven't... I believe you. We stop this right here. Nobody on earth will ever know. And if I was wrong, I’m sorry for what I did."

Sirius leaned forward, intent. "But if I'm right—and I usually am—then you're like most people. You've thought about it. A fleeting moment. Something you shoved to the back of your mind because you were scared of it."

He tapped Remus on the knee, a light, insistent pressure. "Answer me. Just be honest with yourself."

Remus sat there, his hands twisting the duvet until the fabric was nearly knotted. The denial was on the tip of his tongue, a reflex born of years of hiding. But the game was up. There was no point lying to the person currently sitting on the end of his bed.

"Yes," Remus whispered, the word barely audible. "I have."

"Then why are you getting so worked up?" Sirius asked. "Talk to me. Tell me. Say it out loud."

Remus pulled his knees tighter to his chest, burying his chin in the gap between them. He couldn't look Sirius in the eye.

"Because I'm not you," Remus said, his voice gaining strength, though it still wavered like a violin string pulled too tight. "You can afford to be interesting. You can afford to be reckless. I just have to be safe. I like girls, Sirius. I really do. But then..."

He took a ragged breath, the shame burning in his chest.

"But then there's the showers. Most of the time I feel nothing. I just wash. But occasionally, I feel something and I shut it down immediately. I have to." He looked up, his eyes wet. "Because being a werewolf is already a life sentence. If I'm... if I'm queer too... then it's not just the wolf that's wrong. It's me. It means I'm not a normal person fighting a bad thing. It means I'm just... broken. All the way through. And saying it out loud makes it true. It proves there's nothing left to save."

"You feel nothing most of the time in the showers, surrounded by it," Sirius pointed out, his tone pragmatic. "That doesn't sound like something a poof would say, does it? A real queer would be having a field day."

"I suppose," Remus admitted, dropping his hands. "I don't leer. I don't want random blokes. So if I'm not that, then... why did I want it? Why did I want you to do it?"

"Did you want me to do it?" Sirius challenged, a smirk playing on his lips. "Seems to me you thought I was playing a prank. You were calling my bluff. Seems to me you didn't really think I would."

"No, I guess not," Remus said, shaking his head slowly, the realization dawning on him. "You offered. And you're right—I thought you were playing a prank. I thought you were having me on. When I told you to get on with it, I was calling your bluff. I assumed that no matter what I said, you’d take the piss. I was trying to force a situation where you couldn't. But then... you did. And I, I liked it."

"Newsflash, Moony. Listen closely," Sirius said, his voice dropping, intent and serious. "That doesn't make you bent. Actions don't make you what you are, don't you see? A bender can go his whole life and not touch another bloke. Does that make him straight? Of course not."

Remus let out a long, shuddering breath. The panic that had been clawing at his throat suddenly loosened its grip, slipping away into the musty air of the dormitory.

"It suppose it makes sense," Remus admitted, the corners of his mouth twitching upward despite himself. "But then... why does it feel like it should? Why does it feel like I've broken some fundamental law of the universe? If it doesn't make me bent... then what does it make me?"

"Listen, Moony, if you need a label, you'll have to come up with one," Sirius said, waving a hand dismissively. "We're brothers, you, me, and James."

"And Peter."

"Yes, I suppose, Peter too," Sirius sighed with exaggerated tolerance. "I know how you all look up to me as the coolest of the group. A role model, I'm sure. Well, brother, here's what I am. I'm a bloke who loves girls. I'm also a bloke who likes cocks. I'm not confused, Remus. I know myself, and I am fine with who I am. It simply happens to be nobody's business."

Remus exhaled, a long, shuddering breath. "Thank you. For that, and this."

"Get some sleep," Sirius said, swinging his legs over the edge of the mattress. The bedsprings groaned in the quiet. "You look like the Giant Squid used you for a punchbag."

He reached for the heavy velvet curtain.

Remus caught his wrist. Sirius stopped, looking back, his dark hair falling over his eyes.

"You've done that before," Remus said. The memory of Sirius's mouth, the absolute confidence of it, sat heavily in his stomach.

Sirius looked down at Remus’s hand on his arm, then up at his face. He met Remus's eyes with a steady, unblinking gaze. His face remained smooth, pale, and impassive in the gloom.

"Yes."

Remus swallowed. The word hung there, undeniable.

"With who?"

Sirius pulled his wrist away gently. He looked past Remus, toward the darkened window where rain lashed against the glass. The playful arrogance slid off his face, replaced by something harder and more closed.

"It isn't my place to say," Sirius said quietly.

"I won't tell anyone," Remus promised, his voice low. "You know I won't."

"I know." Sirius turned back to him, his expression set. "But the person doesn't want it known. So it won't be. I'm not hiding it because I'm ashamed, It's simply between he and myself. The same guarantee you have pal. Nobody will ever hear about it from my lips."

Remus smiled. A small, crooked expression, yet genuine. He understood secrets better than anyone; he lived inside one. "I get it," he said, his voice low. "Sorry for prying."

Remus looked at Sirius’s hands, resting loosely on his knees, and felt that strange, pulling curiosity again. It went beyond simple physical pleasure, Sirius had made it feel safe. Allowed.

"If this is truly private," Remus started, then stopped. His heart hammered a frantic rhythm against his ribs. He forced himself to breathe. "Can I... would it be okay if... I... I'd like to try it. What you did. I mean. Is that okay?"

Sirius watched him for a long moment, his head tilted to one side like a large, inquisitive dog. Then he smirked, a flash of white teeth in the dim light.

"You want to suck on my bell-end?"

The crudeness of it made Remus flush hotly, his eyes darted away from Sirius’s face to the safety of the bed hangings. Words failed him. Admitting it aloud felt like a betrayal of his own composure.

Fingers gripped his chin—firm, warm, insistent. Sirius turned his head back, forcing him to look. Grey eyes bored into him, demanding, yet soft.

"Say it, Moony," Sirius said, his voice dropping to a rough whisper. "Be okay with what you want. And then yes. You may."

Remus swallowed hard. He looked into Sirius's eyes and saw only a challenge. He took a shuddering breath and forced the words past the lump in his throat.

"I want to suck ... it."

The moment the words were out, the world held steady. The ceiling held fast overhead. Sirius smiled, a slow, satisfied curve of his lips, and let go of Remus's chin. "Good enough Mooney."

"See?" Sirius said as he began to remove his bottoms. He shoved them down, kicking them away to tangle in the sheets. "Was that so difficult?"

He sat back, legs apart, confident. "Your turn," he said, nodding at Remus's waist. "Take em off."

Remus slid his pajama bottoms down and kicked them free. He began to fold them but Sirius snatched the bundle and hurled the pants into the corner.

Remus sat back against the pillows, and crossed his legs.

Sirius rose to his knees and peeled his t-shirt off then paused, "In for a penny, Mooney. Take it off." He said as he tossed his shirt into the growing pile.

Remus pulled his shirt over his head. He began to fold it and stopped when his roaming gaze landed on Serious' expression. Into the pile it went.

Sirius shuffled forward on his knees. "Go on. You can touch it if you like. Don't be intimidated by its regal magnificence. He gestured at the hangings. "Nobody can hear or see and nobody will ever know. LOOK at me Remus. Nobody will ever hear it from me. This is your chance. If you were ever curious, ever had a fantasy of fiddling with -a less perfect specimen to be sure, this is the time.”

- "Mooney" (corrected to **Moony**)

**CORRECTED PROSE**

The Great Hall was warm and loud, a fortress against the storm hammering the enchanted ceiling. The air smelled of toasted bread and oatmeal. Remus Lupin sat at the Gryffindor table, feeling surprisingly solid. It was two weeks until the moon—that golden window where the scars were faded silver and the bone-deep ache hadn't yet returned. He felt capable. He felt hungry.

He stacked a third pancake onto his plate.

"So," Sirius Black was saying, gesturing with a piece of toast, "I told Flitwick that the collision of the two desks was a commentary on the socio-economic struggles of goblin rebellion architecture."

"He was probably crying because you singed his eyebrows, Padfoot," James Potter replied.

But James's voice lacked its usual boom. He was hunched over his plate, his face a pale, waxy shade under the messy black hair. He wasn't eating. He was just staring at a sausage as if it were a poisonous beetle.

"I didn't singe them," Sirius argued. "They just... receded. In fear."

Peter Pettigrew giggled, spraying crumbs across the table. “Better you than me,” he wheezed.

Remus watched James. He looked wrong. There was a sheen of sweat on James's forehead that had nothing to do with the heat of the room.

"Prongs?" Remus asked quietly. "You alright?"

James blinked, looking up slowly. "Yeah," he said, his voice rough. He rubbed a hand over his face. "Actually... no. I've been feeling a bit under the weather. Since last night."

"You should go to Pomfrey," Remus said immediately.

"Nah," James waved a hand. He swallowed hard, his throat clicking. "Just need to... get some air."

The smell of the sausages seemed to suddenly saturate the space between them. James's face shifted from pale to an alarming shade of green.

"James?" Sirius leaned in.

James clapped a hand over his mouth. He doubled over.

A stream of vomit exploded across the golden platters of sausages and bacon. Splatter hit the goblets and dripped onto the tablecloth. The smell of bile cut through the warm breakfast aromas like a knife.

For a second, nobody moved.

Peter let out a high-pitched squeak and scrambled backward, nearly tumbling off the bench. Sirius shot upright, his knees catching the underside of the table and jostling it, which sent a jug of pumpkin juice tipping over. Orange liquid spread toward the edge, mingling with the mess.

"Bloody hell, Prongs!" Sirius yelped, pinching his nose. "That was visceral!"

James gasped, spitting onto the mess. He looked up, eyes watering, utterly humiliated. "Told... you..."

He swayed dangerously.

"Right, that's it," Remus said, sliding out from the bench and grabbing James's arm. "Sirius, grab his other arm. Hospital Wing. Now."

"This is why I stick to toast," Sirius muttered, though he was already gripping James's shoulder, supporting the weight with surprising gentleness. "Come on, you disaster. Let's get you to bed."

"I'm fine," James mumbled weakly, wiping his mouth on his sleeve. "Just... a bug."

"Yeah, a bug that just murdered the Gryffindor breakfast," Sirius said, steering him toward the doors. "Madam Pomfrey is going to love this."

As they moved away, a sharp swish echoed behind them. Remus glanced back. Professor McGonagall had descended from the high table, her lips pressed into a thin line. With a brusque wave of her wand, she vanished the mess in an instant. A twist of her wrist replaced the soiled tablecloth with a fresh, white one, and a new platter of sausages popped into existence, steaming and innocent, as if nothing had ever happened.

Remus turned back to the cool air of the entrance hall. It wasn't fatal, but it was messy enough to ensure James wouldn't be leaving the Hospital Wing for a week. Madam Pomfrey didn't mess around with stomach bugs.

Remus felt a strange, sharp pang of guilt. James was miserable. But Sirius would be pacing the common room within the hour, climbing the walls with boredom and worry. He would need a distraction. And Remus, for the first time in weeks, felt strong enough to be one.

The Hospital Wing smelled of liniment and starch. Madam Pomfrey took one look at James's greenish pallor and ushered him into a curtained bed with the efficiency of a field medic.

"Stomach flu," she diagnosed, waving her wand over a glass of potion that turned a violent shade of purple. "Drink this. And don't let him leave that bed for three days, minimum."

James swallowed the potion with a grimace and collapsed back against the pillows, looking pathetic.

"Try not to die, Prongs," Sirius said, backing away as Pomfrey shooed them toward the door. "We'll bring you homework."

"Don't you dare," James mumbled, already pulling the sheets up.

They sprinted down the marble staircase, taking two steps at a time. The corridors were emptying; the bell was due to ring any second.

They slid through the door of the Transfiguration classroom just as the clock on the wall began to chime. Professor McGonagall was writing on the blackboard, her back straight as a rod. She didn't turn around, but her chalk paused for a fraction of a second as they tiptoed to their usual seats in the back row.

Remus sat down, breathless. A second later, Sirius dropped into the chair beside him.

The chair on the other side of Sirius remained empty. James's usually chaotic desk was clear—no half-finished essay, no doodles of Snitches, no stack of Chocolate Frog cards.

McGonagall turned around, her spectacles glinting. She scanned the room, her gaze flickering over the empty seat.

"Mr. Black," she said. "I see Mr. Potter has decided to spare my classroom the contents of his stomach."

She turned back to the board, tapping it with her wand. The words HUMAN TO ANIMAGUS: THE PHYSIOLOGY OF TRANSFORMATION appeared in elegant, swirling script.

"Open your books to page 213," she commanded.

The rest of the day seemed to drag. Without James to whisper jokes across the History of Magic aisle or pass illicit notes during Charms, the castle felt strangely large and echoing. The other students went about their business, but the gap at the Gryffindor table was impossible to ignore.

By the time the final bell rang, the gray light of afternoon was already fading into a dreary twilight. Remus and Sirius walked back to the tower in a comfortable silence, though Sirius kept tapping his wand against his leg, a rhythmic, impatient beat.

The common room was a welcome relief of warmth and noise. The fire was roaring in the grate, casting flickering orange shadows over the worn armchairs. It was crowded, students sprawled on the rug finishing essays or huddled in corners gossiping.

Remus claimed his usual seat near the fire, sinking into the worn velvet. He opened The Dark Forces: A Guide to Self-Protection, grateful for the distraction. The words on the page blurred slightly—he was tired, but it was a normal tiredness, not the bone-crushing exhaustion of the week before.

Sirius dropped into the chair opposite him, throwing his legs over the armrest. He pulled out a roll of parchment and a quill, but instead of writing, he spent ten minutes trying to see if he could balance the inkwell on the end of his nose.

"It's going to tip," Remus said, not looking up from his chapter on counter-curses. "And you're going to stain the rug, and Filch will make you scrub it with a toothbrush."

"You have no sense of adventure, Moony," Sirius mumbled, the inkwell wobbling precariously. "It's about balance. Focus. Inner peace."

"It's about making a mess."

The portrait hole swung open with a creak, letting in a draft of cooler air. Peter Pettigrew scrambled through, looking flushed and breathless. He craned his neck, spotted them by the fire, and hurried over, dodging a pair of first-years playing Wizard's Chess.

"Well?" Sirius sat up so fast the inkwell slid off his nose and landed safely in his lap with a soft thud. "Is he still alive? Did he survive the Great Sausage Massacre?"

Peter collapsed onto the rug between their chairs, wiping sweat from his forehead. "He's asleep. Pomfrey gave him a Sleeping Draught."

"Poor Prongs," Sirius said, though a grin was tugging at the corner of his mouth. "Finally, some peace and quiet."

"He looks awful," Peter added, pulling a squashed bag of Bertie Bott's Every Flavor Beans from his pocket. "Pale as a sheet. She says he's severely dehydrated. She wouldn't let me stay—she said he needs to rest. He won't be up for dinner, and she said she's keeping him overnight. Maybe two days."

"Two days?" Sirius raised his eyebrows, tossing a bean into his mouth and grimacing—boiled cabbage flavor. "For a stomach bug? She just likes the drama of it all."

"She said he needs to be monitored," Peter mumbled, chewing rapidly. "She wasn't happy when I tried to wake him up to say hi. She practically shoved me out the door."

Dinner had been uneventful. The food was bland, the conversation sparse, and they finished quickly. They headed up to the dormitory to change, and by ten o’clock they were preparing for bed.

The room was filled with the usual sounds of the evening—buckles clinking, robes hitting the floor. Sirius was stripping off his clothes with aggressive, jerky movements. He pulled his shirt over his head with a disgruntled groan. Peter had turned his back to the room, changing with his usual frantic modesty.

Sirius turned, spotted him, and let out a short, sharp, barking laugh.

"Oi, Wormtail," Sirius crowed, leaning against his bedpost with a predatory grin. "Where's your knob? Did you forget to do that bit last time you transformed back into a human?"

Remus flinched. "Keep your voice down," he hissed, crossing the room. "Do you want the whole school to hear?"

"Hear what, Moony? About Peter's teeny weenie? It's not news, mate," Sirius said, stepping casually toward him. "He's all thumbs."

Peter flushed a deep, miserable red, snatching his pajama bottoms and yanking them up with trembling hands. "Oh, very funny," he muttered, his voice high and tight. "I haven't heard that one a thousand times. I especially love the part where you act like a jerk."

"Please," Sirius said, waving his hand as if to banish a gnat. "This is all in good fun."

"Yeah, your kind of fun," Peter said, marching toward his bed. He yanked the curtains shut with a violent snap, ignoring them both.

Sirius shrugged, looking unrepentant. He moved silently toward his bed, sliding off his trousers and tossing them into the pile against the wall. He sat on his bed, scratching his chin absently.

Remus snuck away from the commotion and took his place at the foot of his own bed. The usual sounds of the dormitory began to fade as the other students settled in for the night. Sirius remained awake, chipping away at a physics essay, his brow furrowed in concentration.

"You're not going crazy, are you?" Remus asked in a low, cautious voice.

Sirius glanced at him, one eyebrow up. "Why in the name of everything holy would you ask me that?"

"You're just sitting there."

"Yeah? I'm thinking. You should try it sometimes." Sirius turned back to his parchment, but Remus could see the amusement dancing in his eyes.

"I just... you have this intensity when you do things," Remus said, picking at a loose thread on the duvet. "Like you're trying to set the world on fire."

"That's called ambition, Moony."

"It's called being a firecracker with a short fuse."

Sirius stuck out his tongue at him mockingly. Then he snapped his book shut, pushing it aside. "I'm bored."

"Take a nap."

"Impossible. My mind's racing about half a mile an hour."

Remus shrugged, hoping his nondescript posture would ward Sirius off. He picked up his book, but Sirius was already on his feet.

"What do you say we play a game of wizard's chess?" Sirius suggested. "There's one in the corner over there. You can't tell me you're too tired. Sorry about James, but look on the bright side: for the next few days, it's just the two of us."

"And Peter," Remus pointed out.

"Peter's a total nonentity. It's like he isn't even there." Sirius began assembling the board, the stone pieces clicking crisply. "Come on, Moony. I need someone to score on. My ego is taking blows just sitting here without someone to annoy."

"Fine." Remus stood and walked over, eyeing the’-’ board. "But I'm white."

Sirius scoffed. "Suit yourself. I’ve got the power of the dark lord on my side anyway."

"That's not how it works."

"It is when I'm opponening you, Moony." Sirius regarded him with a curious tilt of his head. "You seem tense."

"I have a lot on my mind," Remus admitted, barely listening.

"Forget it. Leave your troubles at the door. Business as usual—only without James and his pointles bits of refuse strewn everywhere. We'll continue the war against the forces of darkness... the forces of Harry Potter, I mean."

Remus managed a weak smile. "I think I'd prefer to continue the war against your bishop."

"What, game over?" Sirius’s smile widened. "No chance. Deal me in."

It was late by the time the game ended. Remus had won, mostly due to Sirius’s sheer impulsiveness and desire to cheat. They moved back to their beds, this time with the lights off. The dormitory was quiet, save for the rustle of sheets and the regular, echoing rhythm of Peter’s petty snores.

Across the room, Sirius stirred. "Remus?" his voice was just above a whisper, slicing through the darkness.

Remus didn't answer. He kept his eyes fixed on the distant clouds of dust dancing in the faint moonlight that seeped through the window.

For a long time, Sirius remained silent. Then he said, "I bet you miss him."

"He only left for a few hours."

"Yeah, well. The room feels different without him. The silence is weird."

"It's just the night air," Remus said, ignoring the connection. "The castle’s cold."

"True." Sirius yawned, a loud, chomping sound. "Anyway, I'm going to bed. Seal your curtains before a giant spider crawls under your covers and tries to rub its abdomen on you."

"Goodnight, Sirius."

"Night, Moony."

Remus waited until he was certain the boy had fallen asleep. He lay there for a while, listening to the distant wind, listening to the castle settling on its foundations. And then, almost without meaning to, he found himself thinking about the way Sirius had sounded—smiling into the darkness, his voice echoing remorselessly against the stone walls. He shifted, trying to cool down. The heat of shame returned, a thick gag that choked him.

The next morning the reality hit. The common room was buzz with the latest drama, James Potter’s disastrous stomach ailment now the hot topic.

"At least we still have Prongs to keep us entertained," Sirius declared, leaning back in his chair. "Shame about the sausages, though. Those were excellent."

Liliane Potter, her arms folded, glared at Sirius from across the table. "You’re being terribly insensitive. James is normally very thoughtful and careful with others' feelings. His outdoorsy side must be what did him in."

"I swear," Remus murmured. "How can you let him talk like that?"

"Because it's funny," Sirius said simply. "Plus, half of what I say is true."


As they all began to pack up their things for the morning lessons, Remus felt the weight of Sirius’s gaze on him, when he turned to find the other boy’s face pinched with an inscrutable expression.

"You're trying to lurch away," Sirius said. "Trying to run away? Running from what, Moony?"

"Nothing," Remus said, walking up the stairs to the boy's dormitory.

"It's on your face. That war you're fighting. I can see it." Sirius advanced toward him, his eyes warm.

Remus turned. "It's just the baby. I'm tired, Sirius. And everything hurts."

"Listen, you 봐야 and look after yourself. You're morally upright, go to sleep once in a while. Eat the stupid porridge." He reached out and grabbed Remus by the shoulder, pulling him into the doorway of their room. "You should try to relax more. Maybe take a bath. A big, deep, hot bath, with bubble bath and your own little rubber ducky."

"Thank you," Remus said, his voice dead.

The boys filtered back around them, creating a noise and activity that made it hard to hear. Sirius shrugged and moved on, leaving him alone.

Heading back downstairs he ran into James Potter, pale and hunched, lurching toward the infirmary with his teeth chattering, a handkerchief held to his mouth.

Remus halted. "Jesus, James."

"I'm alive," James croaked, looking up and managing a bit of a grin. "Finally discharged. Late last night in fact,’ but I’m still a little dizzy."

"You look like you've been gnawed on by a bear."

"You sound like a mum."

"Shut up and go to camp."

James glared at him. "Don't be a brat." He continued on. "Although, I’m feeling a little little peckish now. What’s for lunch?"

Remus watched him go.

Nobody knew, yet. None of them. Remus would keep it that way as long as he could. Sirius was trustworthy, but even he had loose lips, especially with friends. And James—whatever had happened— James had to think it was a joke, just another prank between two close friends. That was the only way it could go. That was the way things were supposed to be.

But walking away, turning his back on the still-teetering James Potter, Remus realized that neither he nor Sirius would be seeing the world the same way again. The same morning sun shone, the same task concerned their coming transformations, the same bubbling tension simmered below the surface of their manic, frantic lives—but something had been irrevocably changed. A new law lay riding over the old laws.

Until the next full moon now only days away he would be with them every evening in the dormitory, and nobody would see or hear anything unusual. But when the wolf returned, it would not be the only monster anyone had to worry about in the darkness. More and more, Remus wondered if the monster were actually them; him and Sirius, and what they had known.

"There's chocolate," he said as Pierre Black joined him.

Remus winced. "Please don't. You do it all the time, your mother's voice." He licked his lips. The thought of Sirius’s breast, swelling and hard under her thin, translucent blouse... Remus shuddered. So how do you distract yourself? He asked himself.

"That's why the Lupin sheets are open," Sirius said, eyeing him.

"Closed," he counterered.

"With the curtains pulled, okay? Look, Moony, when you can't sleep, what do you--?"

"Quiz me."

"On what?"

"Anything."

"Darn. Well it's your turn to talk. Your pair turn;TableModel and I have been yapping away." He shuffled, then seated himself at a desk, picking up a pen. "Write me something."

Remus knelt by the bed, slid back the heavy velvet coverlet and clambered in. Flipping on a small light by the bed he rummaged through his belongings until he had a clue of paper. "How about a story?" he asked.

"Sure. Something we can laugh at. Tell me a funny story, Moony. Let's see..." Sirius thinking, then. "About a guy who built his house in the woods to get away from people, but found he had to build another house inside it to get away from the snakes. Or a dragon. The devil, whatever. I don't remember the rest."

"If it has snakes in it, it's not funny, it's the truth."

"What truth? You don't live in the woods."

Remus licked his finger. The parchment of the page was covered in red ink. "Just tell about the poor bastard in the house."

Sirius sipped his pumpkin juice and grinned. "Alright. Let's start there. Tell me about the guy and his house. What sort of guy?"

He looked Jenuinely interested, leaning over to listen. Remus shrugged and started to pen.

The Secret of her Underwear

This customer, this opposite sex creature, so thin she could almost disappear, clipped on the line, was a new breed of diseased animal. The wasp.
